nativeRange.habitat: The Emerald Crab inhabits tropical western-Atlantic back-reef environments, spending the day tucked into caves, crevices, and live-rock rubble, then emerging at night to forage across reef flats, coral branches, and turtle-grass beds.

decorPreferences: mature live rock with caves, ledges and crevices for daytime hiding
a well-established reef tank with stable parameters and some natural algae growth to graze on
rubble zones and sand-bed margins for foraging at night
single-crab territories — multiple Mithraculus in one tank tend to fight

diet.notes: The Emerald Crab is an opportunistic omnivore and scavenger best known for being one of the few reef-tank animals that will consistently graze bubble algae — including Valonia ventricaria and Ventricaria ventricosa — along with hair algae and other nuisance algae, while also cleaning up uneaten meaty foods and detritus. The diet is commonly supplemented with dried seaweed or nori, sinking pellets, and small amounts of meaty seafood such as mysis or chopped shrimp. Keeping the crab reliably fed is the single most important husbandry consideration: a hungry Emerald Crab is widely reported to shift from algae grazing to picking at LPS polyps, soft corals, snails, and even sleeping fish.

goodWith: most medium and larger reef fish that can avoid a small crab
established reef tanks with natural algae growth to graze on
SPS-dominant reefs (less commonly picked at than LPS)
tangs, wrasses and other algae-friendly tankmates
snail- and hermit-heavy cleanup crews in larger tanks (with plentiful food)
avoidWith: other Emerald Crabs in the same tank (highly intraspecifically aggressive)
small slow-moving fish, especially gobies and blennies sleeping on the rockwork at night
small ornamental shrimp and snails when the crab is underfed
delicate LPS, zoanthids and soft-coral frags in tanks without enough algae
any system recently treated with copper-based medications
wrasses, triggerfish, large hawkfish and pufferfish that will eat the crab

imageCandidates.notes: Mark Loch; 4288x2848. Tack-sharp in-situ reef shot of a vibrant green Emerald Crab with the sculptured green carapace and diagnostic white-tipped (spooned) chelae clearly visible; whole animal in frame.
Jan Ebr & Ivana Ebrova; 2048x1942. In-water full-body dorsal view of a richly green individual on live rock, showing the bumpy carapace and green claws; slight underwater haze but crab is in focus.
Alex Doyle; 1536x2048. Sharp clear dorsal shot of a small algae-camouflaged specimen showing the sculptured green carapace; out-of-water hand shot kept for variety.

summary: The Emerald Crab is a small Caribbean reef crab widely kept in saltwater cleanup crews for its ability to graze bubble algae. Generally considered reef-safe when well fed, though adult specimens have been widely reported to pick at LPS corals, soft corals, or small fish when food is scarce.
careNotes: One of the most popular saltwater cleanup-crew invertebrates, primarily because it is among the very few reef-tank animals that will reliably eat bubble algae (Valonia and Ventricaria). The Emerald Crab is broadly considered beginner-friendly — it is generally regarded as hardy, tolerant of normal reef parameters, and straightforward to feed. The major caveat is size-related opportunism: a well-fed crab is broadly considered reef-safe, but adult specimens (particularly larger males with robust claws) have been widely reported to pick at LPS polyps, zoanthids, soft corals, snails, and to ambush sleeping small fish at night when algae and supplemental food run short. It is generally advised to keep no more than one per tank, as Mithraculus sculptus is highly aggressive toward its own kind, and to supplement the diet with nori, mysis, and a sinking pellet a few times a week even in algae-rich systems. Like all marine invertebrates, the Emerald Crab is intolerant of copper-based medications and rapid parameter swings; drip-acclimate slowly when introducing it to a new tank.
breedingNotes: Breeds via planktonic larvae with roughly two zoeal stages (~2 days each) plus a megalopa stage of 3–9 days before settlement. Captive rearing is widely regarded as very difficult to practically impossible in standard reef setups due to filtration/skimmer losses and the need for appropriate planktonic foods; documented laboratory survival is well under 20%. Effectively not bred at the hobbyist level.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Manifest-supplied LiveAquaria root resolved to this Emerald Crab product page via search. Provided baseline husbandry parameters (max ~2.5 in / 6.3 cm, 72–78°F ≈ 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), reef-safe-when-well-fed status, Caribbean origin, and copper intolerance.

additional.name: Reef2Reef Wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 and could not be accessed; swapped to other reef-aquarium care guides per playbook fallback rule.

additional.name: Aquariumbreeder (Shrimp and Snail Breeder)
additional.notes: Detailed husbandry guide: ~6 cm carapace, 24–28°C, pH 8.0–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, KH 8–12, 10 US gal (~40 L) minimum with a one-per-40-L density limit, 2–4 year lifespan, very difficult captive breeding, depth to 55 m, and explicit reef-risk-when-underfed warning covering SPS, mushrooms, zoanthids, sponges, snails and small fish.

additional.name: AquariumStoreDepot — Emerald Crab care guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (~6.3 cm), 24–28°C, pH 8.0–8.4, SG 1.025, 20 US gal minimum, 2–4 year lifespan, semi-aggressive temperament with males more aggressive, and explicit 'hungry emerald crab will nip soft corals and polyps' caveat.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Mithraculus sculptus)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Mithracidae — current placement after splitting of Majidae — order Decapoda; synonyms Maia sculptus, Mithrax minutus, Mithraculus coronatus), Caribbean / Gulf of Mexico / Bahamas-to-Brazil range, depth to ~54 m, back-reef habitat, and morphology (~4 cm green carapace with white-tipped chelae).

crustacean.moltingNotes: Molts roughly once a month in healthy adults; juveniles more often. Tends to hide for a day or two around the molt and is vulnerable while the new exoskeleton hardens. Stable salinity, calcium, magnesium and iodine support successful molts; sudden parameter swings are the most commonly cited cause of failed molts. The shed exoskeleton can be left in the tank as a calcium and chitin source.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Planktonic larvae pass through roughly two zoeal stages and a megalopa stage before settling. Rearing them in a standard reef tank is effectively impossible, as filtration and protein skimmers remove the larvae before they can develop and appropriate planktonic foods are rarely available in home systems. The Emerald Crab is not practically bred at the hobbyist level.
